text.title: STAR Interview Framework Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the STAR Interview Framework methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale. Hard rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every competency in the rubric has 5 behavior-anchored levels; collapsing to 3 (poor/ok/great) loses the resolution needed for hire-bar arguments.
rule.rationale: Five-level anchors keep inter-rater agreement above the operational target; three-level scales collapse 'near-bar' and 'on-bar' into one bucket and force reviewers to fight over a single threshold.

rule.statement: Calibration session MUST include ≥2 interviewers scoring the same transcripts; a one-person calibration is theatrical.
rule.rationale: Single-author rubrics encode the author's biases without surfacing them; running a second scorer through the same evidence is the only way to detect them.

rule.statement: Anchors describe observable behaviors, not traits; 'shows ownership' is rejected, 'drove the post-mortem to written action items with named owners' is accepted.
rule.rationale: Trait-language anchors invite re-interpretation per candidate; behavior-language anchors pin the evidence the reviewer must cite.

rule.statement: Inter-rater agreement target ≥0.6 (Cohen's kappa or equivalent) on a pilot cohort before the rubric is published; otherwise rewrite the disagreement-heavy anchors.
rule.rationale: Publishing an uncalibrated rubric exports the disagreement into hiring decisions; the pilot gate is the only place to catch this cheaply.

rule.statement: Debrief contract is published with the rubric: who writes first, how dissent is recorded, decision rule (consensus / hiring-manager / vote).
rule.rationale: Without a debrief contract, the loudest voice wins the room; explicit contract neutralizes seniority effects on hire decisions.
